categories.name: JavaScript Programming
categories.description: Language specifics, DOM interaction, and event handling.

topics.name: Event Propagation
topics.description: The mechanism of how events travel through nested DOM elements.

questions.question_text: What is the term for the default behavior where an event moves from the most-nested element 'up' to the least-nested element (like the document)?
questions.explanation: The movement of events from the inner-most element to the outer-most container (document) is referred to as 'bubbling'.
questions.hint: Think of air rising in water.

source.title: A simplified explanation of event propagation in JavaScript.

questions.question_text: If events start in the 'outer-most' element and move 'down' to the most-nested element, what is this process called?
questions.explanation: According to the source, when events move from the outer container down to the nested target, it is referred to as 'trickling'.
questions.hint: It is the opposite of bubbling.

source.title: A simplified explanation of event propagation in JavaScript.

questions.question_text: Which JavaScript method is used to halt the bubbling of events up through the DOM?
questions.explanation: The 'e.stopPropagation()' method halts the bubbling process, preventing the event from firing on parent elements in the stack.
questions.hint: The method name describes exactly what it does to propagation.

source.title: A simplified explanation of event propagation in JavaScript.
source.section: How to use event propagation to your advantage

questions.question_text: True or False: Because elements on a web browser are nested rather than covering each other up, a click on a nested 'a' tag also clicks on its parent row, the table, and the document.
questions.explanation: Elements do not cover each other; they are nested. Therefore, a click on a child element is also technically a click on all its parent containers all the way to the document.
questions.hint: Think about the stack of elements being clicked simultaneously.

source.title: A simplified explanation of event propagation in JavaScript.

questions.question_text: Why might 'e.preventDefault()' fail to stop a parent element's onClick event from firing when a child element is clicked?
questions.explanation: preventDefault() only stops the default browser behavior (like a form refreshing or a link navigating). It does not stop the event from bubbling up the DOM stack to parent elements.
questions.hint: Distinguish between 'browser default action' and 'event bubbling'.

source.title: A simplified explanation of event propagation in JavaScript.

choices.text: Because it is only compatible with form elements.
Because it stops the event from reaching the document but not the immediate parent.
Because it handles default browser actions rather than the event propagation stack.
Because it can only be used in React, not plain JavaScript.

learning_cards.title: Event Bubbling Path
learning_cards.front: Where does event bubbling eventually end if it is not stopped?
learning_cards.back: It moves all the way out to the 'document', the complete container that holds everything in the browser.

source.title: A simplified explanation of event propagation in JavaScript.
